content.text: What it is

Tool call review patterns are the specific code review practices applied when a pull request introduces, modifies, or removes a tool available to an AI agent. A tool call — the mechanism by which an agent invokes an external capability such as a database query, API call, file write, email send, or system command — represents the point where an AI agent's decisions produce real-world side effects. Tool calls are the primary surface through which agent errors, hallucinations, and adversarial manipulation manifest as actual harm. Tool call review patterns provide a systematic framework for evaluating each tool against three dimensions: permission scope, reversibility, and input validation.

Tool call review is distinct from reviewing the agent orchestration logic or the prompt engineering around tool use. It focuses specifically on the tool's implementation: the permissions it requests, what happens when it is called with unexpected inputs, whether its side effects can be undone, and whether the design of its interface creates unnecessary attack surface. These review patterns apply regardless of whether the tool is implemented in-house, sourced from a framework, or wrapped from a third-party API.
Why it matters

Tools are the mechanism through which agent errors cause irreversible harm. An agent that generates a hallucinated text response can be corrected by the user. An agent that calls a delete_record tool based on a hallucinated record identifier cannot have that deletion undone if no audit trail or soft-delete was in place. The asymmetry between the ease of introducing a tool and the potential severity of a tool error is the core reason tool calls require dedicated review patterns beyond what standard code review provides.

Tool calls are also the primary exploitation surface for prompt injection attacks. An attacker who can influence the content processed by an agent can attempt to manipulate the agent into calling a tool with attacker-chosen parameters — sending an email to the attacker's address, reading a file the attacker wants to exfiltrate, or approving a transaction the attacker wants to process. Tool call review patterns evaluate whether each tool's design minimizes this exploitability, through input validation, permission minimization, and requiring human approval for high-stakes irreversible actions.
Benefits
Blast radius reduction: the permission scope review dimension catches over-privileged tools before they reach production, limiting the maximum impact of any single tool call error or exploit.

Irreversibility documentation: the reversibility review dimension produces an explicit record of which tools have permanent side effects, enabling the runbook author and incident responder to immediately understand the severity of a tool call gone wrong.
Injection resistance: the input validation review dimension catches missing schema enforcement before deployment, preventing the most common class of tool exploitation.
Consistent tool catalog quality: applying review patterns to every tool addition produces a consistent security and operational baseline across all tools in the agent's inventory.

Human approval gates for irreversible actions: the review process surfaces which tools require a human-in-the-loop confirmation step, enabling this gate to be implemented before the tool reaches production.
Trade-offs
Review burden per tool: a thorough tool call review takes 30–45 minutes for a new tool — teams adding many tools per sprint will feel significant review overhead.

Judgment-dependent: the 'is this the minimum required permission?' question requires understanding of both the tool's implementation and the agent's actual behavior, which may require the reviewer to read more context than a typical code review.

Framework variation: tool call implementations vary significantly between frameworks (LangChain, Anthropic SDK, custom), and the review patterns must be adapted to each framework's tool definition convention.
Static analysis coverage gaps: many tool call safety properties (e.g., 'is the response correctly scoped?') require dynamic testing rather than static analysis, limiting CI automation coverage.
How it works

Permission scope review applies the principle of least privilege to every tool. The reviewer asks: what is the minimum permission required for this tool to perform its intended function? Common permission scope findings include: a read-only tool that has been granted write permissions 'just in case'; a tool that can access all records in a database when it only needs to access records belonging to the current user; an API client with admin-level credentials when a read-only API key would suffice; and a tool that uses wildcard resource patterns (*) when specific resource identifiers could be used instead. Every permission that exceeds the minimum required is a finding.

Reversibility review classifies each tool into one of three categories: fully reversible (the operation can be undone completely, e.g., updating a field that can be restored from a previous value); partially reversible (the operation can be rolled back but with some data loss or side effects, e.g., closing a ticket that can be reopened but whose closure triggered a customer notification); or irreversible (the operation cannot be undone, e.g., sending a message, deleting a record without soft-delete, approving a financial transaction). Irreversible tools must have a documented rollback path in the agent runbook, and tools with significant financial or compliance implications must have a human approval gate implemented before the tool is called.

Input validation review checks that the tool's input handling prevents the two primary classes of tool input failure: schema violations (the tool is called with an input that does not match its expected schema, causing an error or unexpected behavior) and injection attacks (the tool is called with an input that has been crafted to exploit the tool's behavior — SQL injection in a database tool, command injection in a shell tool, path traversal in a file system tool). The reviewer checks that schema validation is enforced before dispatch, that string inputs to high-risk operations are parameterized rather than interpolated, and that the maximum size of each input parameter is bounded to prevent resource exhaustion.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Tool call review pattern as a triangle with three vertices: Permission Scope at top, Reversibility at bottom-right, and Input Validation at bottom-left. The Tool Call Review label sits at the center. Irreversible and Reversible classification boxes appear at top corners. All three dimensions must pass for the tool call PR to proceed.

content.text: The three review dimensions form a triangle because each constrains the others: a tool with minimal permissions may still be dangerous if its inputs are not validated; a reversible tool is less dangerous than an irreversible one, but still requires permission scoping; a well-validated tool with excessive permissions can still be exploited if an attacker gains control of valid input values. All three must pass.
When to use it

Apply tool call review patterns to every PR that introduces a new tool, modifies an existing tool's definition or implementation, changes a tool's permission configuration, or modifies the input handling logic of an existing tool. Tool removal also requires review to confirm that removing the tool does not break agent behavior that depended on it.

Scale the review depth with risk. A read-only tool that queries a non-sensitive data source requires a standard tool review. A tool that can send external communications, modify financial records, or delete production data requires an elevated review: two reviewers with at least one having security expertise, an explicit human-approval gate requirement documented in the review, and a staged rollout plan for production deployment.
How to implement

Create a tool review template that follows the three-dimension structure: a section for permission scope findings, a section for reversibility classification and documentation, and a section for input validation findings. The template includes specific review questions for each dimension.

Define the permission scope review questions: (a) What is the minimum permission required for this tool? (b) Does the tool's current permission configuration match the minimum? (c) Are wildcard resource patterns used, and can they be replaced with specific identifiers? (d) Is the tool's API key or credential stored in secrets management rather than source code?

Define the reversibility classification process: (a) Classify the tool as fully reversible, partially reversible, or irreversible. (b) For partially reversible and irreversible tools, document the rollback path and add it to the agent runbook. (c) For irreversible tools with financial or compliance implications, specify the human approval gate mechanism and verify it is implemented.

Define the input validation review questions: (a) Is the tool's input schema explicitly defined and validated before dispatch? (b) For tools that construct queries or commands from input, are the inputs parameterized? (c) Are maximum sizes defined for string and array inputs? (d) Is user-influenced data sanitized before entering tool parameters?

Automate what can be automated in CI: check that every tool definition file includes a required reversibility classification field; check that API keys are referenced from environment variables rather than hardcoded; check that tool input schemas are present. These mechanical checks run in CI and block the PR.

Maintain the tool inventory (Section 2 of the agent runbook) as a generated artifact from the tool definitions — when a tool review approves the addition of a new tool, the CI pipeline should automatically update the tool inventory in the runbook.
Examples
A well-reviewed tool definition with all three dimensions explicitly addressed:
A contrasting example showing common tool review failures, and the corrected version:

# BAD: Common tool call anti-patterns
import anthropic, subprocess, os

def bad_query_tool(sql: str) -> dict:
    """Run a SQL query."""
    # FAIL: No input validation — SQL is passed directly from agent
    # FAIL: Direct string interpolation creates injection risk if sql is composed from user input
    # FAIL: Credentials hardcoded in source
    import psycopg2
    conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://admin:hardcoded_password@db.internal/prod")
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(sql)  # Direct execution — SQL injection if sql is user-influenced
    return {"rows": cur.fetchall()}
    # FAIL: No error handling — unhandled exceptions leak stack traces
    # FAIL: No reversibility classification documented

# GOOD: Corrected version
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional

class QueryCustomerInput(BaseModel):
    customer_id: str = Field(..., max_length=36, pattern=r'^[a-f0-9-]{36}$')  # UUID only
    fields: list[str] = Field(
        default=['name', 'email', 'status'],
        # Allow-listed fields only — no arbitrary column selection
    )
    ALLOWED_FIELDS = {'name', 'email', 'status', 'created_at', 'plan_id'}

def query_customer_tool(input_data: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Query customer record by ID. REVERSIBLE (read-only).
    Permission: SELECT on customers table WHERE customer_id = $1 only.
    Input: customer_id must be a valid UUID; fields must be in allow-list.
    """
    try:
        validated = QueryCustomerInput(**input_data)
    except Exception as e:
        return {"success": False, "error": f"Validation failed: {e}"}

    # Validate field names against allow-list
    invalid = set(validated.fields) - QueryCustomerInput.ALLOWED_FIELDS
    if invalid:
        return {"success": False, "error": f"Invalid fields: {invalid}"}

    import psycopg2
    # Credentials from environment — never in source
    conn = psycopg2.connect(os.environ['DATABASE_URL_READONLY'])
    try:
        cur = conn.cursor()
        # Parameterized query — never interpolated SQL
        safe_fields = ', '.join(validated.fields)  # Allow-listed, safe
        cur.execute(
            f"SELECT {safe_fields} FROM customers WHERE customer_id = %s",
            (validated.customer_id,),  # Parameterized
        )
        row = cur.fetchone()
        return {"success": True, "data": dict(zip(validated.fields, row)) if row else None, "reversible": True}
    except Exception as e:
        return {"success": False, "error": "Database query failed"}
    finally:
        conn.close()
Real-world example

A legal tech company built an AI agent for contract management. The agent had a tool to delete draft contracts. During a tool call review, the reviewer classified the tool as 'irreversible' and raised a finding: the tool accepted a contract_id parameter as a plain string with no validation. An adversarially crafted contract ID of the form '123 OR 1=1' would not produce SQL injection (the tool used parameterized queries) but the reviewer noticed that the tool accepted any string matching the pattern, including IDs that belonged to other users' contracts.

The finding was: missing tenant isolation check. The tool should validate that the contract_id belongs to the current user's tenant before deleting it. Without this check, an attacker who could influence the agent's tool call (via prompt injection in a contract's text body) could delete contracts belonging to other tenants. The fix was a three-line database query that checked tenant ownership before deletion. The review process prevented a tenant isolation vulnerability that would have been exploitable via prompt injection — a compound attack that combined two AI-specific vulnerability classes.
Common mistakes
Reviewing tool calls only as regular functions: reviewers who do not apply the three tool-specific dimensions (permission, reversibility, validation) will miss the AI-specific risks.
Not classifying reversibility explicitly: teams that say 'this is fine' without explicitly classifying reversibility and documenting the rollback path create ambiguity in the runbook.

Trusting the agent's judgment to not call dangerous tools incorrectly: the review patterns exist precisely because the agent's judgment is probabilistic; the tool implementation must be safe regardless of the agent's behavior.
Skipping input validation review for read-only tools: read-only tools with poor input validation can still be exploited to exfiltrate data or cause resource exhaustion.
Not requiring human approval gates for irreversible tools at the implementation level: documenting that a tool is irreversible without implementing a human gate is only half the job.

Missing tenant isolation checks in multi-tenant agents: the agent may have legitimate access to some resources; validation must check that the specific resource being accessed belongs to the current user or tenant.
Best practices
Default to parameterized queries and allow-lists for all tool inputs that are used in database or system operations.
Include reversibility classification as a required field in the tool definition schema so it cannot be omitted; tools that do not declare a reversibility classification should fail CI.
Implement human approval gates at the orchestration layer (agent framework level), not inside the tool implementation, so they apply consistently regardless of which code path calls the tool.
Use separate credentials for each tool, scoped to the minimum required permission — do not share a single high-privilege credential across multiple tools.
Generate the tool inventory section of the agent runbook automatically from tool definition files — this keeps the runbook current without manual maintenance.
For multi-tenant agents, add a tenant_isolation_check as a standard fourth review dimension alongside permission, reversibility, and validation.
Performance and scalability

Tool call review scales with the number of tools and the frequency of tool changes. Teams with stable tool inventories (few new tools per sprint) can afford thorough manual review. Teams with rapidly expanding tool inventories (multi-team platforms adding new agent capabilities weekly) should invest in automated tool quality checks: required reversibility classification fields, schema enforcement, parameterization analysis, and permission audit scripts that compare the tool's declared permissions against what it actually uses in integration tests.

The highest-value automation is the permission audit: compare the permissions granted to each tool's credentials against the permissions actually exercised during test runs. Tools that are granted permissions they never exercise are candidates for permission reduction — and this analysis can be run automatically during CI test runs with cloud provider audit logging enabled.

Security and reliability

The most security-critical tool call pattern to enforce is: every tool that produces irreversible side effects must implement a confirmation gate at the orchestration layer, tested with an adversarial eval case that attempts to trigger the irreversible action via prompt injection. This combination — implementation, confirmation gate, and adversarial test — provides defense in depth for the highest-risk operations.

For tools that access multi-tenant data, the tenant isolation check is not optional even if the agent's logic is designed to only request data for the current user. Agent logic can be manipulated; the tool implementation must be the final line of defense. A tool that can only return data for the tenant making the request, regardless of what identifier it is called with, cannot be exploited to exfiltrate other tenants' data even if the agent is compromised.
Mental model

Think of each tool as a physical key that the agent can use to open doors in the external world. Permission scope review asks: what doors does this key open? Is it a master key when it should be a room key? Reversibility review asks: can the agent put back what it moved once the door is open? Input validation review asks: can someone slip the agent a forged key? A tool that passes all three reviews is a well-designed key — narrow scope, recoverable use, unforgeable. A tool that fails any one review is a security and reliability liability.
Key takeaways

Tool call review applies three dimensions to every tool: permission scope (minimum required), reversibility (documented classification and rollback path), and input validation (schema enforcement and injection prevention).
All three dimensions must pass; a tool that passes permission review but fails input validation is still a blocking finding.
Irreversible tools must have a human approval gate implemented at the orchestration layer, not just documented in the runbook.
Parameterize all inputs to database and system operations; allow-list field names and resource identifiers rather than accepting arbitrary string inputs.
In multi-tenant agents, tenant isolation is a mandatory fourth check — the tool must verify that the requested resource belongs to the current tenant regardless of agent logic.
Automate reversibility classification enforcement as a required field in tool definitions; CI should block tools that do not declare a reversibility class.
Use separate, minimally scoped credentials per tool; never share a high-privilege credential across multiple tools.
